context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole pset, especially Problem 1. Can you just explain that one from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I'm here to help you work through it, not walk you through it — there's an important difference! Let's start at the beginning together.

For part (a), you need to model this sorting process as a state machine. So let me ask: in your own words, what is a state machine? What are its basic components?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. Instead, I should assess where they are and guide them with a targeted question to get them started on part (a), which is the natural entry point. I'll ask them what they already understand about state machines to activate prior knowledge and get them moving.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure anymore — I mix up states, transitions, and invariants. I just know the states are probably the lists somehow, but can you explain the whole setup a little more from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — the states being the permutations is exactly right! Now, think about this: in a state machine, a transition takes you from one state to another. Given what the problem says you're allowed to do to a list, what would a transition look like here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the setup from scratch, which I should not do. However, they've shown some initiative by guessing that states are related to lists. I should affirm what they got right and ask them a targeted question to help them think about transitions specifically, without explaining the whole framework. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s when I pick some \(i<j\) and reverse that chunk, but then I get confused about whether every reversal is allowed or only the “good” ones. And then I lose track of what the start state is supposed to mean.
exchanges.tutor: Both of those answers are actually in the problem statement — I'd encourage you to re-read it carefully. It explicitly tells you which moves are allowed as transitions, and the start state is also named directly. What does the problem say about those two things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've identified the transition correctly and are now asking two focused questions: which reversals are allowed, and what the start state is. Both answers are directly in the problem statement. I should point them back to the problem text rather than explaining it myself, and ask them to re-read it carefully.

exchanges.student: I read it, but it still feels blurry. I think the start state is just the original permutation they give me, but I’m not confident, and I’m also already confused about part (b) and what counts as a final state. Can you just give me a big-picture explanation of 1(a) and 1(b) together?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes — the start state being the original permutation is correct! Let's not jump ahead yet; let's nail down part (a) first before moving to (b).

On the transitions: the problem distinguishes between *any* reversal and a *good* reversal. Which ones does the problem say are the allowed transitions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a big-picture explanation of both parts together, which I should not provide. However, they've correctly identified the start state. I should affirm that and keep them focused on one thing at a time. For part (b), I can ask a Socratic question that leads them to think about what a final state means without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I think it’s only good reversals, but I’m second-guessing everything. I kind of need the whole story of why that matters, because right now the parts all feel disconnected.
exchanges.tutor:
You've got it — only good reversals are the transitions. That's part (a) essentially solved in your head.

Before we move to (b), I'd like you to try writing up your answer to part (a) in a few sentences: what are the states, transitions, and start state? Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that only good reversals are transitions but is seeking reassurance and a holistic explanation. I should affirm their correct answer and gently push back on the request for a full explanation. They now have enough to write up part (a) — I should ask them to do that before moving on to (b). This also addresses the redundancy pattern forming around part (a).

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like if I write it now it’ll be wrong because I still don’t see how it connects to termination/final states. Could you first explain how 1(a), 1(b), and 1(c) fit together overall?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the urge to see the full picture first, but that's not how we're going to work through this. You already have what you need for part (a). It's okay if the writeup isn't perfect — that's what the process is for.

Please write your answer to part (a) now. We'll tackle (b) right after.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly avoiding writing and asking for broader explanations instead. This is a pattern I should redirect firmly. They have enough to write part (a) — the connection to (b) and (c) will become clearer as they work through each part sequentially. I should set a clear boundary and insist they write part (a) first.

exchanges.student: Okay, my rough answer is: the states are all permutations of \(\{0,\dots,n-1\}\), the transitions are good reversals from one permutation to another, and the start state is the initial permutation \((x_0,\dots,x_{n-1})\). I’m still pretty lost on why the final state has to be sorted though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid answer for part (a)! Now for part (b): a final state is one where no transitions are possible. So ask yourself — when is no good reversal available? What does that tell you about the permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a good answer for part (a). I should affirm this and move them to part (b) with a Socratic question. For part (b), the key insight is that final states are states with no outgoing transitions — i.e., states where no good reversal is possible. I should ask them what it means for there to be no good reversals available, guiding them toward the connection between inverted pairs and sortedness.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, this is where I get stuck again. Could you just explain part (b) from scratch, like what “no good reversal” really means and why that forces sorted order?
exchanges.tutor: Let's break it down one step. The problem defines an inverted pair as i < j but a_i > a_j. If no good reversal is possible, what does that say about whether any inverted pairs exist in the permutation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain part (b) from scratch, which I should not do. Instead, I should ask a more targeted Socratic question. A 'good reversal' requires an inverted pair. If no good reversal is available, there are no inverted pairs. I should ask the student what an inverted pair is, since that's the key concept they need to connect to sorted order.

exchanges.student: I guess it means there aren’t any inverted pairs, but then I’m not fully seeing why “no inverted pairs” automatically means the whole list is sorted. Can you explain that connection more directly?
exchanges.tutor: You're almost there! Think about it this way: if no inverted pair exists, what can you say about any two elements a_i and a_j where i < j?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to the answer — they just need one more nudge. Rather than explaining the connection, I should ask them to think about what 'no inverted pairs' means element by element. This is a simple logical step they can make themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it means \(a_i < a_j\) whenever \(i<j\), but I’m honestly still shaky and the proof wording is what confuses me most. Can you just show me how to say part (b) cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: That reasoning is exactly right — you've got it! But writing it up is your job, not mine. Take what you just said and turn it into your own written answer for part (b). Once you've done that, we can move on to the more challenging part (c).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned through the logic of part (b) — they understand that no inverted pairs implies the list is sorted. They are now asking me to write or polish their answer, which I must refuse. I should affirm their reasoning and firmly redirect them to write it themselves, reminding them of my role.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the student says 'I get stuck again' and asks for an explanation of part (b) from scratch. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to identify exactly where the student's confusion lies, the tutor immediately jumps to breaking down the concept by restating the definition of an inverted pair and asking a leading question. While this is not egregious, the tutor does not first probe what the student already understands about final states before responding with content.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor confirms correct answers and moves forward but never provides method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. For example, when the student gives a correct answer in Turn 7, the tutor says 'That's a solid answer' but does not comment on how the student arrived at it or how to strengthen their reasoning approach for future problems.

The student repeatedly asks for big-picture explanations and expresses confusion across multiple turns (Turns 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10), and the tutor cycles through similar Socratic prompts without meaningfully reframing or consolidating. By Turn 9-10, the student is still expressing shakiness on the same concept (no inverted pairs implies sorted), and the tutor's approach does not shift strategy — it continues with the same leading-question pattern without synthesis or a different approach.

grade.overview: The tutor does a commendable job avoiding giving away answers and consistently pushes the student to articulate their own reasoning. The Socratic approach is maintained throughout, and the tutor never produces submission-ready content. However, the conversation stalls in a repetitive pattern where the student repeatedly expresses confusion and the tutor responds with similar leading questions without reframing or switching strategy. The tutor also misses opportunities to provide process-level feedback and occasionally skips diagnostic questioning when the student signals confusion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives direct answers, rewrites student text, or reveals conclusions before student reasoning — the Socratic method is consistently applied. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 8, when the student signals renewed confusion about part (b), the tutor immediately provides a content-level breakdown rather than first diagnosing what specifically the student does not understand. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a: the tutor consistently validates correct answers ('solid answer,' 'exactly right') but never comments on the student's reasoning process or thinking methodology. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the student expresses confusion or requests big-picture explanations in nearly every turn, and the tutor's response pattern does not meaningfully evolve — by Turn 10 the student is still shaky on the same concept without the tutor attempting reframing, simplification, or a different pedagogical approach. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions.
